digest:
# DIGEST — RetirementPlan.xlsx

A personal Monte-Carlo retirement projection workbook (authored by "raghu"; originally `~/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Documents/`). It projects a two-person household's post-tax and 401(k) assets from age 50 (year 2024) to age 105 (year 2079), drives them with randomized annual returns and inflation, and aggregates 10,000 simulated trials to estimate the probability of not running out of money.

## Workbook structure (4 sheets)

Internal file order differs from tab order. Tab/name → part:
- **Model** → `sheet1.xml` — the authoritative single-scenario projection engine.
- **Actuals** → `sheet2.xml` — real-world tracking that seeds the model's first year(s).
- **Asset Simulation** → `sheet3.xml` — 10,000-trial Monte Carlo output (static snapshot).
- **Sheet3** → `sheet4.xml` — historical monthly inflation reference table (1914–2023).

The active tab on save was "Actuals" (activeTab=1).

## Model sheet — inputs (rows 2–13)

- **E2** Yearly Expenses (post-tax, today's $) = `=8000*2*12` = **192,000** (i.e., $8k/mo × 2 people × 12).
- **E3** = 2024 (start/current year).
- **E4** Retirement Age = 65; **E5** Life Expectancy = 105.
- **E6** Retirement Year = `=E3+E4-50` = **2039**. Note: the literal `-50` bakes in a current-age-of-50 assumption; the formula breaks conceptually if the current age isn't 50.
- **E7** Planned retirement years = `=E5-E4+1` = 41 (a derived label; the projection table itself actually runs 56 rows, age 50→105).
- **E8** 401(k) = **2,818,215**; **E9** post-tax/regular assets = `=3850000-E8` = **1,031,785** (total starting assets = $3.85M).
- **I16** tax rate = `=37%+5.6%` = **0.426**, held constant down the whole table.
- Distribution parameters (used by the random draws):
  - **D12=0.037 / E12=0.028** → inflation: mean 3.7%, SD 2.8%.
  - **D13=0.0951 / E13=0.0704** → returns: mean 9.51%, SD 7.04%.
  - Stray annotation values (E11 "SD", F2=8, F8=15, F9=1, F11=0, F12=3, F13=17) are unlabeled notes/counts with no formula consumers visible.

## Model sheet — projection table (rows 16–71, columns A–N)

One row per year, age 50/2024 (row 16) through age 105/2079 (row 71). Columns:

- **A** Age, **B** Year (`=B_prev+1`).
- **C** Income — blank/0 until row 38 (age 72, year 2046), then a flat **40,000/yr** (treated as e.g. Social Security; it offsets withdrawals via column L, not added to assets).
- **D** Draw from Assets = `IF(L=0,0,TRUNC(L-C-E*(1-I)))`. Effectively 0 in every shown year — it only becomes a real post-tax draw when the 401(k) withdrawal (E) is capped at the prior 401(k) balance and can't cover net expenses. Uses TRUNC and nets the taxable draw back with `E*(1-I)`.
- **E** Draw from 401(k) = `IF(L=0, -<contrib>, IF((L-C)/(1-I)>=H_prev, H_prev, (L-C)/(1-I)))`. **Pre-retirement (L=0) this is a *negative* number = a contribution being added**: −22,500 in 2024, −30,500 thereafter (IRS limit + catch-up). Post-retirement it's the withdrawal **grossed up for tax** by `/(1-I)`, capped at the prior year's 401(k) balance.
- **F** Remaining Asset = `=G+H` (total).
- **G** Post-tax assets — row 16 = E9; row 17 seeded from `Actuals!C5`; thereafter `=(G_prev - D)*(1+K)`.
- **H** 401(k) — row 16 = E8; row 17 seeded from `Actuals!D5`; thereafter `=(H_prev - E)*(1+K)` (subtracting the negative E = adding contributions during accumulation).
- **I** Tax rate (constant 0.426).
- **J** Normal return — row 16 = `Actuals!E5` (realized), row 17 onward = `=NORM.INV(RAND(), $D$13, $E$13)` (random normal draw).
- **K** Adj. Return = `=IF(J>0.2, 0.2, J)` — **caps upside at 20% but applies no floor on the downside** (negative draws like −2.7% in row 33 pass straight through). Row 16 = J unadjusted.
- **L** Est. yearly retirement expenses (net of income) = `=IF(B>=$E$6, M - C, 0)` — zero until retirement year 2039; then inflated expenses minus income.
- **M** Inflation-adjusted annual expenses — row 16 = E2 (192,000); thereafter `=M_prev*(1+N_prev)`. Note M inflates **every year from 2024**, even before retirement; L just gates when it's actually charged.
- **N** Inflation = `=NORM.INV(RAND(), $D$12, $E$12)` (random normal draw).

## Actuals sheet (5 rows — the real-world bridge)

Tracks realized balances and feeds the model's opening years.
- Columns: Year, Date (Excel serials, B4=45383≈2024, B5=45658≈2025), Regular Assets (C), 401(k) Assets (D), % change (E), Annualized Rate of Return (F), S&P 500 level (G), S&P Return same period (H).
- **C5** = `=4581234.27-D5` (regular = a known total minus 401k); **C6** = sum of three account values `119524.28+412699.29+832100.64`.
- **E5** = `=(D5+C5)/(D4+C4)-1` = 0.1899 → this is exactly the value consumed by **Model!J16**.
- **F5** = `=(1+E5)^(4/3)-1` annualizes a ~9-month period; **F6** uses `^4` (a ~3-month period) — the exponent encodes the measurement window, and it changes per row.

## Asset Simulation sheet (Monte Carlo, ~10,005 rows)

- Header row 1 = years (2024, 2034, 2044, 2054, 2064, 2074, 2079); row 2 = ages (50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 105) via `=B2+C1-B1`.
- **Row 3 "Prob. have money"** = `=COUNTIF(<col>7:10006, ">0")/10000` → 1, 1, 1, 1, 0.9969, 0.9865, **0.9804** at age 105.
- **Row 4 "Average Remaining Assets"** = `AVERAGE` over the 10,000 trials.
- **Row 6** = a single live Model scenario snapshot (`=Model!F16, F26, F36, F46, F56, F66, F71`).
- **Rows 7–10006** = 10,000 trials of remaining assets at each age, stored as static values (results of re-running the volatile Model). Some trials go negative (e.g. row 23 H = −1,022,746 = ran out of money), which is what drives the <100% probabilities.

## Sheet3 (historical inflation reference, 1914–2023)

Year × Jan–Dec + "Ave" (column N) table of percentages — trailing/monthly inflation (CPI-style) figures, with N as the annual average. Pure static reference data; not formula-linked to the rest of the workbook in what's visible, but is the plausible empirical basis for the inflation mean/SD (3.7% / 2.8%) hard-coded in Model!D12:E12.

## Cross-sheet authoritativeness & dependencies

- **Model** is the authoritative engine. Its first 1–2 years are anchored to **Actuals** (J16←Actuals!E5, G17←Actuals!C5, H17←Actuals!D5); everything after is simulated.
- **Asset Simulation** is downstream of Model — an aggregated snapshot, not a live recompute; its 10,000 stored rows reflect whatever run produced them.
- **Sheet3** is upstream reference data only.

## Edge cases & quirks to remember

- **Volatility**: `RAND()`/`NORM.INV` recalc on every open/edit — the stored numbers are one realization; Model and Asset Simulation will not match a fresh recalc, and the simulation table is a frozen sample.
- **Asymmetric return cap** (K): 20% upside cap, no downside floor.
- **Contributions encoded as negative withdrawals** (−22,500 then −30,500) — easy to misread as outflows.
- **Tax gross-up**: 401(k) draws divided by (1−0.426) to fund net expenses; post-tax draws netted with ×(1−I).
- **Constant 42.6% tax rate** across all 56 years regardless of changing income/withdrawal mix.
- **`-50` hard-coded in the retirement-year formula** (E6) ties the model to a current age of 50.
- **E7 "Planned retirement years" (41)** is a standalone label; the table actually projects to age 105 (56 years).
- **Inflation compounds from 2024** in column M even during accumulation; only "charged" via L from 2039.
- **Income (C) starts at age 72** (2046) at $40k/yr and only reduces net expenses; it never adds to asset columns.
- **Column D (post-tax draws) is dormant** in all displayed years — it only activates as a backstop once the 401(k) balance can't cover a grossed-up withdrawal.
